name: Dark matter identity
summary: Galactic rotation curves, cluster lensing, the CMB acoustic peak structure, and large-scale structure formation independently require ~26% of the cosmological energy density to be in non-baryonic, non-relativistic, gravitationally interacting matter that does not couple substantially to photons. Decades of direct, indirect, and collider searches have failed to identify the particle (or non-particle) responsible.

characteristic_numbers.note: Dark and baryonic densities are within a factor of 6, despite (presumably) entirely different origins. This 'cosmic coincidence' is itself a sub-puzzle.

principles_stressed.note: Dark matter does not violate any foundational principle; it just requires a beyond-SM particle (or compact object). Its identity remains the largest unsolved BSM consistency question grounded in unambiguous data.

history: Postulated by Zwicky (1933) from Coma cluster velocity dispersion; confirmed by Rubin's rotation curves (1970s); cemented by the CMB. WIMP miracle dominated 1980s-2010s; null results from XENON, LUX-ZEPLIN, PandaX have pushed cross-sections below 10^-47 cm^2 in the 30 GeV range. Axion, sterile-neutrino, primordial-black-hole, and ultralight scalar candidates are increasingly active alternatives.

references.citation: Bertone and Hooper, 'A History of Dark Matter', Rev. Mod. Phys. 90, 045002 (2018)
